title: Random Quran Verse and Random Hadith: Discover the Quran and Sunnah Beyond the Familiar
excerpt: The Quran has 6,236 verses. Sahih Bukhari has 7,563 hadiths. Most Muslims encounter only a fraction. The Random Verse and Random Hadith tools open the full depth of both — one click at a time.
author: QuranBookk
content:
## The Parts of the Quran That Have Been Waiting for You

Stop and consider honestly: how many of the Quran's 6,236 verses have you sat with? Not recited in Salah without comprehension — but actually read, understood, and reflected on.

Most Muslims, even committed ones, return to the same verses their entire lives. Al-Fatihah. Ayat al-Kursi. The last three Surahs. Surah Ya-Sin. Surah Al-Mulk. The verses they memorised as children. The passages quoted in sermons. The familiar ones.

This is entirely natural. But it means that thousands of Quranic verses — verses that Allah revealed, preserved across 1,400 years, and placed in the Book for the benefit of *you specifically* — remain largely unvisited.

The [QuranBookk Random Quran Verse](/quran/random-ayah/english_saheeh) feature opens the full 6,236. One click. One verse. With translation.

## The Verses That Almost No One Quotes

The most impactful moments with the Random Verse feature come from verses outside the familiar top 100. Here is a small sample of the kind of discovery it enables:

**Surah Al-Hashr 59:18**

<AyahBlock
  arabic="يَٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَلْتَنظُرْ نَفْسٌ مَّا قَدَّمَتْ لِغَدٍ"
  translation="O you who have believed, fear Allah. And let every soul look to what it has put forth for tomorrow."
  reference="Surah Al-Hashr 59:18"
/>

This verse, rarely quoted, contains one of the Quran's most direct instructions for self-accounting: *look to what you have put forward for tomorrow.* Not what you intended. What you actually put forward.

**Surah Az-Zumar 39:53**

<AyahBlock
  arabic="قُلْ يَٰعِبَادِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَسْرَفُوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ لَا تَقْنَطُوا۟ مِن رَّحْمَةِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَغْفِرُ ٱلذُّنُوبَ جَمِيعًا"
  translation="Say: O My servants who have transgressed against themselves, do not despair of the mercy of Allah. Indeed, Allah forgives all sins."
  reference="Surah Az-Zumar 39:53"
/>

Note the direct address: *O My servants who have transgressed against themselves.* Allah is speaking directly to the person who has sinned — and the message is not condemnation but one of the most expansive mercy statements in the entire Quran: *He forgives all sins.*

**Surah Al-Inshirah 94:5–6**

The famous promise that hardship comes with ease. What is less known: the verse appears *twice* in consecutive form — *"Fa inna ma'al-'usri yusra. Inna ma'al-'usri yusra."* Classical scholars note that in Arabic grammar, when a definite noun (*al-'usr* — *the* hardship) is repeated, it refers to the same hardship. When an indefinite noun (*yusra* — *an* ease) is repeated, it refers to two different instances. One hardship. Two eases.

These are the kinds of discoveries that emerge from the Random Verse — not new verses, but familiar verses seen fully for the first time, and unfamiliar verses encountered as if for the first time.

## The Random Hadith: 7,563 Narrations of Prophetic Life

The [Random Hadith feature](/hadiths/random-hadith/bukhari) draws from Sahih al-Bukhari — the most authenticated Hadith collection in the world (see the [Authentic Hadith Collections guide](/blog/authentic-hadith-collections-online) for full context on the collection and its significance).

Sahih al-Bukhari contains approximately 7,563 hadiths — covering the Prophet's ﷺ worship, character, daily life, legal rulings, statements about the afterlife, treatment of family, companions, and creation, and much more. Most Muslims are regularly exposed to perhaps 50–100 of them — the most frequently quoted ones in sermons and Islamic education.

The Random Hadith feature surfaces the other 7,400+. A narration about the Prophet's ﷺ physical description. A statement about the etiquette of entering a home. A description of the Companions' practice. A ruling on a question most Muslims have never heard framed in prophetic terms. A description of Paradise that is not among the standard ones quoted.

<HadithBlock
  text="None of you truly believes until he loves for his brother what he loves for himself."
  narrator="Anas ibn Malik (رضي الله عنه)"
  source="Sahih al-Bukhari 13"
/>

This is one of the most famous hadiths in Bukhari — number 13. What about hadith 4,000? Or 6,500? The Random Hadith makes those accessible.

## Making It a Daily Practice: The 60-Second Habit

The most sustainable use of both tools is as a daily micro-practice:

**The morning routine**: With morning tea or coffee, click once on [Random Quran Verse](/quran/random-ayah/english_saheeh). Read the verse. Read its translation. Spend 60 seconds reflecting on what it says. That is all.

**The transition moment**: Before a work session, click once on [Random Hadith](/hadiths/random-hadith/bukhari). Read the narration. Note what it is about. Carry it with you.

**The consistency compound**: At 365 verses per year, a daily Random Verse practice encounters 3,650 verses over 10 years — more than half the Quran's verses encountered deliberately. At 365 hadiths per year, a daily Random Hadith practice covers Sahih Bukhari's unique content in approximately 7 years.

<Callout type="tip">
When a Random Verse or Random Hadith resonates deeply — save it. Explore it further in the [Quran Surahs feature](/quran/surahs/) for the full Surah context, or in the [Hadith collections](/hadiths/hadiths-sources/) for the hadith's chapter and surrounding narrations. A single random encounter can open an entire field of study.
</Callout>

## Low Friction. High Consistency. A Relationship With the Full Book.

The Random Verse and Random Hadith tools are the simplest features on QuranBookk — one click each, requiring no navigation, no search term, no prior knowledge. Their purpose is equally simple: to expand your relationship with the Quran and Sunnah beyond the 50 verses and 50 hadiths that Islamic digital culture perpetually recirculates.

The full Book of Allah and the full Prophetic legacy are there. These tools open the door.

👉 [Get a Random Quran Verse](/quran/random-ayah/english_saheeh) · [Get a Random Hadith](/hadiths/random-hadith/bukhari)
